refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology that implies a specific DP) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design of each capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent designated work zones (operational areas, restricted zones, safety perimeters) in the worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and retrieve worksite spatial data in a format accessible to planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The parent FR is now atomic in intent (capture and represent), but the sub-FRs should each be verified as atomic before design parameter selection.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; maintain zone-feature mappings in a relational schema; provide zone-based query and update APIs.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a voxel-grid occupancy model with semantic labels; support incremental updates from streaming sensor data; provide spatial query primitives (collision checks, line-of-sight, nearest-neighbor) for planning functions.

DP4: Establish a feature-centric event stream: each detected or updated feature (obstacle, hazard, access route) is published as a timestamped event; maintain a queryable event log and current feature state snapshot; allow planning/execution to subscribe to feature changes.

DP5: Represent worksite as a hybrid graph-mesh model: nodes encode feature locations and semantic labels; edges encode spatial relationships and traversability constraints; mesh encodes continuous geometry; support both discrete (graph) and continuous (mesh) queries.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Represent worksite as a hybrid graph-mesh model: nodes encode feature locations and semantic labels; edges encode spatial relationships and traversability constraints; mesh encodes continuous geometry; support both discrete (graph) and continuous (mesh) queries.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between feature acquisition (sensing) and representation: DP5 assumes multi-modal sensor fusion upstream; if sensor modalities change, representation schema may require revision. Mitigation: define a sensor-agnostic feature abstraction layer (IM boundary) that decouples acquisition from representation.

Coupling between worksite safety policy and graph/mesh semantics: safety constraints (e.g., exclusion zones, hazard buffers) must be encoded in edge weights and node labels. If policy changes, model structure may need updates. Mitigation: parameterize safety constraints as separate policy modules that annotate the base graph/mesh.

Coupling between planning/execution consumers and query interface: if planning algorithms require specialized spatial queries (e.g., visibility graphs, Voronoi diagrams), the DP must support extensible query primitives. Risk: over-specification of query API. Mitigation: define a minimal core query set (collision, nearest-neighbor, traversability) and allow consumers to derive higher-level queries.

Mesh resolution and update latency: continuous geometry representation may incur computational cost during incremental updates. If worksite changes rapidly, mesh updates may lag. Mitigation: decouple mesh update frequency from graph update frequency; prioritize graph consistency for real-time planning.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 balances expressiveness (hybrid representation supports both discrete planning and continuous geometry) with modularity (graph and mesh can be updated independently). Coupling risks are primarily at the IM layer (sensor integration, policy binding, query API design) rather than the DP logic itself. Recommended to pair with explicit IM boundaries for sensor fusion, policy annotation, and query dispatch.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite features are static or change slowly relative to planning/execution cycle time. Risk: if features change rapidly (e.g., dynamic obstacles, moving personnel), the model may become stale. Mitigation: define update frequency SLA and implement change-detection mechanisms.

Assumption: A unified coordinate frame can be established and maintained (e.g., via GNSS, fiducial markers, or odometry). Risk: coordinate frame drift or loss of localization reference. Mitigation: implement frame validation and re-calibration protocols.

Assumption: Feature classification taxonomy is well-defined and stable (e.g., obstacle types, hazard categories). Risk: ambiguous or novel features may not fit taxonomy. Mitigation: define a fallback 'unknown' category and implement human-in-the-loop refinement.

Assumption: Planning and execution functions can tolerate model uncertainty and incompleteness. Risk: missing or misclassified features may cause planning failures or safety violations. Mitigation: implement confidence scores and uncertainty propagation in the model; define acceptance thresholds for planning.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formalized and can be encoded as graph/mesh annotations. Risk: informal or context-dependent policies may not translate cleanly. Mitigation: establish a policy formalization process and version control for policy changes.

Assumption: Query latency and throughput requirements are compatible with the chosen representation (graph + mesh). Risk: large-scale worksites or high-frequency queries may exceed performance budgets. Mitigation: define performance SLAs early; consider spatial indexing (e.g., octrees, R-trees) for acceleration.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP5 to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Feature Acquisition & Fusion (upstream DP): multi-modal sensing -> feature abstraction layer. (2) Spatial Representation (core DP): hybrid graph-mesh model with semantic labels. (3) Policy Binding (cross-cutting DP): safety constraints and operational rules annotate the base model. (4) Query & Access (downstream DP): provide minimal core query primitives (collision, traversability, nearest-neighbor) and extensible hooks for consumer-specific queries. This decomposition reduces coupling and clarifies IM boundaries: separate modules for sensor integration, model storage, policy engine, and query dispatcher. Additionally, introduce an explicit 'Model Confidence & Uncertainty' DP to track feature detection confidence and model completeness, enabling planning functions to make risk-aware decisions.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized worksite representation and spatial reasoning engine that maintains and queries the hybrid graph-mesh model of the operational environment

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives sensor data (LIDAR, camera, IMU) from Vehicle to update graph nodes, edges, and mesh geometry in real-time

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Provides discrete graph queries (node locations, traversability paths) and continuous mesh queries (collision geometry, terrain properties) to Vehicle and Teleoperation for motion planning and task execution

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Exports spatial constraints and hazard zones (encoded as graph edges with traversability=false or mesh regions) to Safety Services for constraint validation

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Synchronizes worksite model state with remote operators and other agents; receives updates from distributed sensors; broadcasts model changes for consistency

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for environment representation and spatial reasoning. The hybrid graph-mesh model is a core knowledge structure that supports planning, navigation, and hazard reasoning. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion (Vehicle), tasking output (Vehicle/Teleoperation), and safety constraint export (Safety Services) cross boundaries via well-defined interfaces.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a platform/agent module responsible for actuation and local sensing. Embedding a global worksite model here would couple the vehicle to environment representation, violate separation of concerns, and force duplication if multiple agents need the same model. High diffusion: Teleoperation, Safety Services, and other vehicles would need to query Vehicle for spatial data.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command relay layer. While operators need to query the worksite model for visualization and planning, the model itself should not be owned by the UI layer. Ownership here would create tight coupling between operator interface and spatial reasoning, and would diffuse the model across Vehicle and Safety Services.

Weak fit. Safety Services consumes spatial constraints (graph traversability, mesh hazard zones) but should not own the primary worksite model. Safety Services is a consumer/validator, not the authoritative source. Ownership here would force Vehicle and Teleoperation to query Safety Services for basic spatial queries, creating inverted dependencies and diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 (hybrid graph-mesh worksite model) is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) NGHE is the designated spatial reasoning and planning engine; (2) the model is a shared knowledge structure that multiple agents (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) consume but do not own; (3) containment within NGHE minimizes diffusion by centralizing model updates and queries through well-defined interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert out); (4) this aligns with classical separation of concerns: environment representation is distinct from actuation (Vehicle), UI (Teleoperation), and safety validation (Safety Services).
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck if worksite model updates are high-frequency and network latency is significant. Mitigation: implement local caching in Vehicle and Teleoperation with eventual consistency; use Network Link for asynchronous synchronization.

Risk: Mesh geometry updates from continuous sensor streams (LIDAR) may overwhelm NGHE if not efficiently compressed/decimated. Mitigation: implement mesh simplification and incremental update protocols in the Telemetry interface.

Risk: Multiple agents (Vehicle, remote operators, autonomous planners) may generate conflicting updates to the graph-mesh model. Mitigation: establish a versioning and conflict-resolution strategy; designate NGHE as the authoritative source with write-serialization.

Risk: Safety Services may need real-time access to hazard zones without network latency. Mitigation: replicate critical safety constraints to Safety Services via SafetyAlert; use local caching with periodic validation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable against acceptance criteria: (1) 'A worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features' maps directly to the hybrid graph-mesh model's dual-query capability (discrete graph for feature locations, continuous mesh for geometry); (2) 'Worksite safety plan and operational policies' maps to the SafetyAlert interface exporting traversability constraints and hazard zones. Verification can be conducted via: model instantiation tests (graph nodes/edges populated from sensor data), query response tests (tasking interface returns valid paths and collision geometry), and safety constraint export tests (SafetyAlert correctly encodes hazard zones). Traceability is clear: FR -> DP5 (hybrid model) -> IM (NGHE Solution) -> interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert out).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: EXCELLENT. The allocation of DP5 to NGHE Solution is unambiguous and well-justified. NGHE is designated as the 'Centralized worksite representation and spatial reasoning engine,' making it the natural and sole owner of the hybrid graph-mesh model. The four external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) establish clear input/output contracts: Telemetry feeds sensor data to update the model; Tasking and SafetyAlert export queries and constraints to consumers; Network Link synchronizes state. No ambiguity exists regarding which module owns the model or how it is accessed. Ownership is singular and non-diffused.

diffusion_risks: MODERATE: Telemetry interface ingests sensor data (LIDAR, camera, IMU) from Vehicle to update graph/mesh in real-time. If sensor processing logic (e.g., feature extraction, mesh decimation) is not clearly owned by NGHE, diffusion risk emerges. Mitigation: explicitly assign sensor-to-graph/mesh transformation algorithms to NGHE; Vehicle provides raw sensor streams only.

MODERATE: Network Link synchronizes worksite model state with remote operators and distributed sensors. If synchronization logic (versioning, conflict resolution, eventual consistency) is not centralized in NGHE, diffusion risk emerges across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and remote agents. Mitigation: designate NGHE as authoritative source; implement write-serialization and version control within NGHE; Network Link is a transport layer only.

LOW: Tasking interface exports both discrete (graph queries) and continuous (mesh queries) to Vehicle and Teleoperation. Risk is minimal because queries are read-only and NGHE retains ownership of the model. However, if Vehicle or Teleoperation cache and modify local copies of the model without synchronization, diffusion occurs. Mitigation: enforce read-only access via Tasking interface; use Network Link for any local updates.

LOW: SafetyAlert exports spatial constraints (traversability, hazard zones) to Safety Services. Risk is minimal because Safety Services is a consumer/validator. However, if Safety Services generates new hazard zones that must be reflected in the model, ownership ambiguity emerges. Mitigation: establish clear protocol: Safety Services identifies hazards; NGHE integrates them into the model; SafetyAlert broadcasts updates.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT. The hybrid graph-mesh model is highly contained within NGHE Solution (containment_score: 0.95, diffusion_score: 0.05). The model is a core knowledge structure that logically belongs to a spatial reasoning engine, not to actuation (Vehicle), UI (Teleoperation), or validation (Safety Services). The four external interfaces are well-defined and minimize diffusion: (1) Telemetry is a unidirectional input channel for sensor data; (2) Tasking is a unidirectional output channel for read-only queries; (3) SafetyAlert is a unidirectional output channel for constraint export; (4) Network Link is a bidirectional synchronization channel with clear ownership (NGHE as authoritative source). No evidence of scattered responsibilities or duplicated logic across module boundaries. The DP-to-IM mapping is clean and defensible.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid graph-mesh model with dual-query capability) is a sound and complete solution to FR (capture and represent worksite spatial features). The DP addresses both discrete and continuous aspects of spatial representation: graph nodes/edges encode feature locations, semantic labels, and traversability constraints (discrete); mesh encodes continuous geometry and terrain properties (continuous). The dual-query capability (discrete graph queries for planning, continuous mesh queries for collision detection) directly supports the acceptance criteria ('available to planning/execution functions'). The DP is technology-agnostic and does not over-specify implementation details (e.g., specific graph library, mesh format), allowing flexibility in realization. No logical gaps or contradictions between FR and DP. The DP is verifiable: model instantiation, query correctness, and constraint export can all be tested.

recommended_revisions: CLARIFY SENSOR-TO-MODEL TRANSFORMATION: Explicitly document which algorithms (feature extraction, mesh decimation, node/edge inference) are owned by NGHE vs. delegated to Vehicle. Recommend: NGHE owns all transformation logic; Vehicle provides raw sensor streams via Telemetry interface.

FORMALIZE SYNCHRONIZATION PROTOCOL: Define versioning, conflict resolution, and eventual consistency strategy for Network Link. Recommend: NGHE maintains a version counter; all updates are serialized through NGHE; remote agents receive versioned snapshots; conflicts are resolved by NGHE (e.g., last-write-wins with timestamp validation).

ESTABLISH SAFETY CONSTRAINT FEEDBACK LOOP: Clarify how Safety Services identifies new hazards and how they are integrated into the model. Recommend: Safety Services exports hazard reports to NGHE via a dedicated SafetyUpdate interface; NGHE integrates and broadcasts via SafetyAlert.

DEFINE CACHING STRATEGY FOR LATENCY-SENSITIVE CONSUMERS: Document local caching policies for Vehicle and Teleoperation to mitigate network latency. Recommend: Vehicle and Teleoperation maintain read-only caches of relevant graph/mesh subsets; NGHE broadcasts incremental updates via Network Link; periodic full-state synchronization for consistency validation.

SPECIFY MESH SIMPLIFICATION PROTOCOL: Define how high-frequency LIDAR updates are compressed/decimated to avoid overwhelming NGHE. Recommend: Vehicle performs local mesh simplification (e.g., voxel-based decimation); NGHE receives simplified updates; full-resolution mesh is retained for critical safety queries.

ADD ACCEPTANCE TEST CASES: Extend acceptance criteria with specific test scenarios: (1) Model instantiation from sensor data; (2) Graph query correctness (path finding, traversability validation); (3) Mesh query correctness (collision detection, terrain property lookup); (4) SafetyAlert constraint export; (5) Network Link synchronization under latency/packet loss.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR5 is sound (FR-DP-IM all pass), but recommended_revisions expose critical decomposition gaps at the next level. The hybrid graph-mesh model is architecturally correct, but its realization depends on sub-level FRs that are not yet explicit: (1) sensor-to-model transformation ownership and algorithm decomposition; (2) synchronization protocol with versioning and conflict resolution; (3) safety constraint feedback loop integration; (4) caching strategy for latency-sensitive consumers; (5) mesh simplification under high-frequency updates. These are not minor implementation details—they are functional requirements that must be decomposed to ensure safety criticality (high) is met and diffusion risks (MODERATE on Telemetry and Network Link) are mitigated. Proceeding without explicit sub-FRs risks deferring critical architectural decisions to detailed design, where they become harder to change. ZIGZAG_DOWN to decompose FR5 into sub-FRs that address sensor ingestion, state synchronization, and constraint integration.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Transform raw sensor streams (LIDAR, camera, IMU) into graph nodes/edges and mesh geometry updates with feature extraction and spatial alignment.
FR5.2: Synchronize worksite model state across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and remote agents with versioning, conflict resolution, and eventual consistency guarantees.
FR5.3: Integrate safety constraints (hazard zones, traversability restrictions) from Safety Services into the graph-mesh model and broadcast updates to consumers.
FR5.4: Provide read-only query access to graph (discrete feature/path queries) and mesh (continuous geometry/collision queries) for planning and execution functions.
FR5.5: Compress and decimate high-frequency sensor updates (e.g., LIDAR point clouds) to prevent model saturation while preserving safety-critical geometry.

revise.why: DP5 (hybrid graph-mesh model with dual-query capability) is logically sound but under-specifies the transformation, synchronization, and constraint integration mechanisms. Revise DP5 to explicitly include: (1) sensor-to-model transformation pipeline (feature extraction, mesh decimation, node/edge inference) owned by NGHE; (2) versioned state synchronization with serialized writes and conflict resolution (NGHE as authoritative source); (3) safety constraint feedback loop (Safety Services -> NGHE -> SafetyAlert); (4) read-only query interface with caching strategy for latency-sensitive consumers; (5) mesh simplification protocol (Vehicle performs local decimation, NGHE receives simplified updates). These clarifications will inform sub-level DPs and reduce diffusion risk on Telemetry and Network Link interfaces.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is the sole authoritative owner of the worksite model; all updates are serialized through NGHE to prevent conflicting writes.
Vehicle provides raw sensor streams (LIDAR, camera, IMU) without pre-processing; NGHE owns all feature extraction and mesh generation logic.
Network Link is a transport layer only; synchronization logic (versioning, conflict resolution) is owned by NGHE.
Safety Services identifies hazards and reports them to NGHE; NGHE integrates and broadcasts via SafetyAlert.
Vehicle and Teleoperation maintain read-only caches of relevant graph/mesh subsets; local modifications are not permitted.
High-frequency sensor updates (e.g., LIDAR at 10+ Hz) are decimated locally by Vehicle before transmission to NGHE to avoid saturation.
Safety criticality (high) requires deterministic synchronization and conflict resolution; eventual consistency is acceptable only for non-safety-critical features.
open_risks: MODERATE: Sensor-to-model transformation latency. If feature extraction and mesh generation are computationally expensive, NGHE may not keep pace with high-frequency sensor updates. Mitigation: profile transformation algorithms; implement incremental updates; consider distributed processing if needed.

MODERATE: Network Link synchronization under latency and packet loss. If remote agents experience high latency or packet loss, model divergence may occur. Mitigation: implement versioning and timestamp-based conflict resolution; periodic full-state synchronization; eventual consistency guarantees.

MODERATE: Safety constraint feedback loop. If Safety Services identifies new hazards that must be integrated into the model in real-time, latency in the feedback loop may delay hazard propagation. Mitigation: prioritize safety constraint updates; use dedicated SafetyUpdate interface with low-latency guarantees.

LOW: Caching strategy for Vehicle and Teleoperation. If local caches become stale due to network delays, planning/execution may use outdated spatial information. Mitigation: implement cache invalidation and refresh strategies; broadcast incremental updates; periodic full-state synchronization.

LOW: Mesh simplification protocol. If Vehicle's local decimation is too aggressive, critical safety geometry may be lost. Mitigation: define decimation thresholds; retain full-resolution mesh for safety-critical regions; validate simplified mesh against full-resolution before use.
evidence_needed: Sensor-to-model transformation algorithm specifications (feature extraction, mesh generation, node/edge inference) with computational complexity analysis and latency estimates.
Synchronization protocol specification with versioning scheme, conflict resolution strategy, and eventual consistency guarantees.
Safety constraint feedback loop specification with latency requirements and integration protocol.
Caching strategy specification for Vehicle and Teleoperation with cache invalidation and refresh policies.
Mesh simplification protocol specification with decimation thresholds and validation procedures.

Acceptance test cases for each sub-FR: (1) sensor-to-model transformation correctness; (2) synchronization under latency/packet loss; (3) safety constraint integration latency; (4) cache consistency validation; (5) mesh simplification fidelity.
Safety analysis (FMEA or similar) for each sub-FR to identify failure modes and mitigation strategies.
Performance benchmarks for sensor ingestion rate, model update latency, query response time, and synchronization overhead.
